Home·Journal·Local guide

48 hours in Cape Town

Sipho Ndlovu · Concierge14 June 20267 min read

People come to Cape Town for the beach and never leave it — and honestly, you could do worse. But after fifteen years showing guests around, here's how I'd actually spend a weekend.

The trick to Cape Town is to slow right down. It's a small place, beautifully hemmed in by the mountain on one side and the Atlantic on the other, and almost everything worth doing is within a flat, easy walk. Leave the car. You won't need it until day two.

Day one — find your spot

Start late and start slow. Breakfast on the terrace, then walk the promenade north while it's still quiet — the palm trees, the tidal pool, the early swimmers who've been doing this for forty years. By mid-morning, claim a patch of sand. The water is properly cold; that's the point. You earn the sun by bracing through a swim first.

Lunch is a long, lazy affair, ideally with your feet still sandy. Then back to the resort for the hottest part of the afternoon — a nap, the pool, a chapter of a book you won't finish. The whole day is building toward one thing: the sunset. There is nowhere in Cape Town that does it better, and you've got a front-row seat.

"The first rule of Cape Town is that you never plan around anything except the light."— Sipho Ndlovu

Day two — go a little further

Now use the car. Twenty minutes one way takes you up the cable car to the top of Table Mountain, best done early before the cloud — the "tablecloth" — rolls in. Twenty minutes the other way is Llandudno, a smaller, wilder cove with no shops and no crowds, just granite boulders and very clear water.

If you do only three things beyond the beach, make them these:

  • The cable car up Table Mountain — go for the first car of the morning.
  • A drive over Suikerbossie toward Hout Bay, for the view alone.
  • A fish braai at the harbour, eaten with your fingers, sun going down.

But if you do none of them and never leave your lounger, I won't judge you. The best guests are the ones we barely see, because they've found their spot on the sand and decided that's the whole holiday. Most of the time, they're right.

SN
Sipho Ndlovu
Concierge · Royal Hotel
